speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there at that session we had?

speaker: interviewer
text: In [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: That whole discussion erupted--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I had to lea--

speaker: interviewer
text: --about tapping?

speaker: interviewer
text: I had to leave early.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, what a shame. Were you there?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was crazy. [? They were talking about politics ?] at the interviews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, god.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was such crap. Then suddenly, this argument erupted about the tapping methods of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Today the 16th of July, 1995. The survivor's name is Edith Polgar, P-O-L-G-A-R. The interviewer is Gretta Rusanow, R-U-S-A-N-O-W. The city is Sydney, and the country is Australia. And the interview's going to be conducted in the English language. Camera's rolling the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's July 16, 1995. I'm Gretta Rusanow. And today I'm interviewing Edith Spielmann Polgar in Sydney, Australia. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling and set.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Edith Polgar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-D-I-T-H, P-O-L-G-A-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name, the maiden name, it is Spielmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-D-I-T-H, S-P-I-E-L-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1922, December, on the 12th.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18-- I'm 72 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 72. And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what city and country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baden bei Wien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Baden bei Wien, that's in Austria. I have been born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you spell Baden bei Wien for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Baden bei Wien, B-A-D-E-N, stroke, W-I-E-N,

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you des--

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that to me, Baden bei Wien? Can you describe it for me? What sort of a-- was it a big city, or a town? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, Vienna is a big city from Austria. Next to that is Baden bei Wien. It's a holidays resort. And it's a lovely city. It's about one hour from Vienna. So it's like a connection-- Sydney and Melbourne, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somewhere in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it like somewhere in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to talk louder.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. OK, all right. Was it-- how far was it from Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: An hour, one and a half hour. It depends what you caught, a bus or a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said it was a holiday resort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was its main attraction?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main attraction was we had the casino there and the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a resort where all the [? arthritics ?] or [INAUDIBLE] people go in there to cure themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a spa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's not a spa. It's a beautiful resort, a holiday resort. And in there is that warm water, where the people go in and cure themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's Hugo Spielmann, H-U-G-O, S-P-I-E-L-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's name is July Goldsten. It's J-U-L-Y, G-O-L-D-S-T-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want the maiden name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just their first name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Greta Weisz and Herta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they fit into the-- the family? Are they-- they younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're both younger, yes. Greta is the middle one. Herta is the youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had any brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your parents do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a tailor shop in Baden [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means a street name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] That's means a street name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the tailor shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was quite a nice little shop. My father worked with five or six men in the tailoring. We had a lot of customers from overseas. And I went to school. And I quite often went in to see my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to [NON-ENGLISH] school. It wasn't far from my father's shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A public school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what you learned at school, what subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had just an ordinary subject, nothing else. The only thing that I had the subject was English.

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: English,

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned English straight away when I went to school. I was about in the third year, I think, I learned English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you speak any other languages at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian, because my father was a Hungarian. My mother was from Burgenland and actually from Deutschkreutz. And she was born there. And we lived in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they come to live in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: He met my mother in Austria, and he never went back. He liked Austria .

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have extended family in Austria as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother had a family in Burgenland, two sisters. That time, her mother was alive, too. I really knew her. And [INAUDIBLE] how Hitler came in, they all went to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baden?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge Jewish community, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I couldn't remember on the-- on the numbers. But it was huge and very good communities. There were Neologs, and there were Orthodox. And we were amongst the Orthodox, because my mother was very religious-- my father, too. And I had a very nice childhood there. And we had relations there, too-- aunt and uncle from my mother's side. They called Kohn family. And they had in the [NON-ENGLISH]-- that is a street-- they had a very big textile shop. And they were deported, too. They never came back, none of my family, nobody. Nobody came back except from my father's side, two brothers and we are-- we three sisters. Nobody's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in your spare time when you weren't studying at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I play tennis. I went swimming. My-- our father took us out to the [NON-ENGLISH] to the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. That is not far from Baden. In between Baden and Vienna is very much long mountains, where you can ski, go skiing. And we went a lot there, Saturday, Sunday, ever when we could. He took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the relations between the Jewish community and the secular community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good relations. We had good relations even with the aldermans, my father. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the council, do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Council, yes. And we had excellent relations with all religions. Because when we had Hanukkah, we had Christmas, too. Because all the people we knew, they were half/half. So we were all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they were half/half, what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I say half/half, my father and mother had all religions friends. So when they had their holiday, it was our holiday, too. When we had the holiday, they had a holiday, too. So we were all in good terms, until the time came that we had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember knowing anything about Hitler in the early '30s--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --living in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was a child. My parents never told me. And my father wasn't a politician at all, not at all. So we don't know anything about it. And these last years just-- '36, '37, '38. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you know about '36, '37, '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father told me maybe we'd leave, or we don't know what's going to happen. And he protected us. We pick-- we have been picked up from school and took to school. We never had that before. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is while you were in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Baden. That was in Baden. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him starting to pick you up from school and protect you from--

speaker: interviewee
text: To protect me. Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do-- how did you react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we thou-- I really thought-- and we all thought-- [INAUDIBLE] was just natural, that our parents come for us. You know? He never enter-- how should I say? He was the one who pro-- protected us with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wouldn't tell us why and how. And like children, any other children, like in the former days-- they were not informed like these days, the children. It was different that time. There were children with the-- with the knee [INAUDIBLE] socks and the-- and the clothes, what they had on, the [INAUDIBLE] clothes. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you remember the big sailor things, what they had on. And we were brought up like that. One was different than these days, when all the children knew anything. So that how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a little bit about what would occupy your time as a child, between school and skiing and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was learning for school. And my mother taught me how to cook. And I had to help her, too. And that was [INAUDIBLE] how I occupied.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your sisters close in age to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we were always together. My mother was called [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means, in English, here is coming Mrs. Spielmann with her three children. Because we were all two, two years different. So we were just like little sheeps, you know? So she called us like that. And she has been called-- when we went for walk, or went to the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] is like here, say, the botanic park. And we went there with her nearly every afternoon. And that what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sounds like it was a beautiful place to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. It was very nice, really very nice. I was once back there, because I didn't want to go back at all. And I wouldn't go back, too many old and good memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your house there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, I do. It was in [NON-ENGLISH]. We lived there. And we had a big garden. We had been brought up there. It was nice. It was really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Austria just an hour before the [NON-ENGLISH]. My father brought us to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you the date. I can't remember the date. I can't remember the time. I know it was happened. And we went to my-- where my grandparents lived. Only my grandmother was alive. But all the brothers and sister from my [INAUDIBLE]-- from my father was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Csorne. Csorne. C-S-O-R-N-E, that's Csorne. That's a little village. And we stayed there for a while. Where is this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is next to [NON-ENGLISH] it's called.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Hungary. And after that, we went to leave to Gyor. And Gyor is Hungarian. Called in German, it's called Raab, R-W-- R-A-A-B it's called. And that is in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you by that stage?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would have had quite a good memory of what was going on around you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sometimes yes, sometimes not. It's fading away. So I can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you-- how did your father explain to you that you were going to leave Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- he did, he did that time. We did talk [? among the children ?] what's going to be happen. And we all started all over again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he decide to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the Hitler came in. And he decided to move to Hungary to, suppose, save his family. But he didn't-- he saved his family, in a way, until Hitler came in to Hungary. And from Hungary, we went to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I don't want to leave your life before the war just yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to know a little bit about your life in Hungary from 1938 onwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to-- to my matriculation. And then after that, the Jews couldn't go to the [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't take them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went and learned millinery and did that for four years. When I finished, then I met my husband. And we went up to live to Budapest. Where did you start to study millinery?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gyor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So what year did you start studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you start studying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I can't remember on the right-- on the right year. It was about-- [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come back to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I just can't remember. I tell you. I have to look at a-- I can't remember what year it was. I really can't remember. Anyhow, I learned that for four years. And then when I finished, I went to live to Budapest to-- stayed there for two years. And I worked with my husband together. And after that, we went back to Gyor. And at that time, already started with the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewer
text: You met him in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about how you met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I was working, did my [INAUDIBLE] millinery, he worked in a very big shop where he was a manager. And I went with different things there, all the time. And we met there. And from then on, we were meeting. And that's what is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe him to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe him to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a real gentleman, tall, with glasses, [INAUDIBLE], and a very nice gentleman, all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so, you married him when?

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December on the 7th, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearly 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's young. And where was your family, by this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: My fam-- in Hungary, also, in the same city everything. Everybody in the same city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you continue to study after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. I was working then what I learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after, when we wanted to establish ourselves, they were all content to go and come to Hungary. And then he had to go to the army. But is-- it wasn't the real army, where they put the Jewish people. It was more the working army. There they put them. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about your wedding? Your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wedding?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '41, it was already the war everywhere. So I didn't have a white wedding. I had a very nice wedding. And we had about 100 people. [? I had ?] my wedding, with my family and all the friends and my parents' friends, and so on. And it was beautiful. And we were very happy together. But they were already very hard times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the early years of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they took my husband away--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in end of '42 .

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can we talk a little bit about-- the war began in 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to know, before we start talking about your experiences in the late--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the late part of the war, what happened in the early part of the war, say, from 1939 onwards? When did you first start to feel [? that way? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '39, we went to-- I have already been in Hungary. And in '40, well, everybody was afraid, because Hungary and Germany-- I mean, Austria and Germany was all on the border. So everybody felt the war is here, and they will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody was very unhappy. And we were very unhappy, too. Because we youngsters who wanted to go ahead and do something, we couldn't do anything. Because everybody was afraid. What going to be happened? And it was happened. Yes, but at the time, you didn't know what was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did everybody know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody knew that if the war comes next to Hungary, that the Germans will march in and that will be the end of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear from your family in Austria, after Anschluss?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Anschluss, they were all went to the concentration camp. We had letters from them where they were writing to us, goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your father try to move again from Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Wasn't any time. There was no time, whatsoever. You couldn't get in or out, nothing. Couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able to continue working?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way, yes. I was continue, because I had to live from something. My husband was already taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was already a few years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a few years later. It was in '4-- late '41, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. No, I know. But if-- but 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, '39. Right, yes. I was-- yes, I was working. I was working in my trade, what I learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there restrictions, already, on your life then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so your husband is taken away, yes? He joins the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '42, beginning of '43. Then he came home. They all took them away--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for a few months. And they came home. And in '43, they took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, all right. I wanted to spend more time on that period beforehand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that you were involved in a Zionist organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mizrachi. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you involved in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mizrachi is a Zion organization where, most likely, the [NON-ENGLISH] people--

speaker: interviewer
text: The religious people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Religious people. And yes, I was joining them. And I worked with them very hard, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their ideology?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to Israel. But there was no time to go to Israel for us anymore. Because [? early on ?] everything was closed. So we couldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to the organization, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the organization, a few people went away. They were taken away, too, and everything. So the organization just went to-- to next to nothing. Because nobody was game enough to go there or do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because everybody was always on our steps. Where you go? What you do? So we couldn't do anything. And were your sisters still at school, at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were able to continue their education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were. Yes, until they took them away-- until we went to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when you-- when you got married, where did you live? Did you live near your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in Gyor. And I lived in [NON-ENGLISH]. We had a little flat there, what we rented. Because we couldn't afford to buy anything. Because we didn't-- we didn't have any money. And we lived there until we went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so while your husband was away, how long was he away for?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went away from there, and he went away for a long, long time. Only I could see him every three or four months. And when they took him out to Germany, I couldn't see him again, never again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you told me that they took him in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, '43. He came back. They came out, again and again. And in the end of '43, they took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like while he was away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he with the Hungarian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he with a labor battalion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Definitely. They took him to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So tell me about your life while your husband-- as a newlywed, your husband is taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very sad. Because I had to keep up the flat and went home to live with my parents and worked until the time came they took us to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you know about what he was doing in the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew was that he was going a labor work, to build homes, and the trains, and everything. Every hard work, they did, very hard work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very upset. And they took the shop away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they take the shop away from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Jewish couldn't have any shops anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '44. So we didn't do anything-- couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up until '44, your husband was taken away. But how did it affect the daily life of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard. Very painful, very hard, a lot of crying, a lot of upsetting, because we knew it was come-- what was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was nothing they could do about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't do a thing about it-- a thing about it. We couldn't do anything. Because the Germans were already in-- in Hungary. And one German soldier told my father that, Mr. Spielmann, don't think that we all like that. I had to go, otherwise they would have shoot me and my family, too. We are not animals, he said to my father. But we had to go. And we had to do our duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father said, where are the others? Why don't you do something? He said, we couldn't. Because they have been shooting quite a few. So that was the situation that everybody wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the Jewish community in-- in that town react?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Jewish community couldn't go together. We couldn't go even to the temple, because they didn't allow anything to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you-- if you were a religious family, how did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: At home if we were game enough to light the candles or anything. Because we never knew when [INAUDIBLE]-- when will come somebody and do something to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about keeping Kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no keeping Kosher, because you couldn't get anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: That went on about for four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in end of-- beginning of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up until '44, were you allowed to still go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was I allowed to?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody went?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they stop you from going?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't stop me. But we didn't went. Because we knew when we went, they do something to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is even before 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was a old Rabbi, Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH] was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was living where the synagogue is. And even him, they took him away quite a few times, and let him out, and put him-- put him back. So nobody wanted to go. And nobody could go even if you wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We might-- we'll leave this tape here.
